CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Tt will be remembered that two gold medals and cor-
tificates were offered as prizes in connection with the
recent Drogheda Industrial Exhibition tc the drapery
establishments in Louth and Meath that had purchased
the largest amount of manufactured goods from
January 1st to September 1st, 1905, one gold medal and
certificate for competition amongst those whose valua-
tion was above, and the other for those whose valuation
was below £95. - The result was surprising and instruc-
_ tive. Mr. Thomas Matthews, tailor, outfitter, draper,

boot and shoe warehouse, etc., of Dunleer, though he
~ could have competed in the section confined to those
whose valuation was under £25, won the first prize.
" This result is very significant, and ‘shows like a flash

what one distributor, if he were a man of earnest good:
~ will in the cause of Irish industry, could do. This com-
petition was open to Louth and Meath, counties that
contain such towns as Drogheda, Dundalk, Navan

. Dunleer, where Mr. Matthews has his shop, is
illage in County Louth with a population of only

480. How is it that a draper in a village of 480 popu-
lation was able to beat the drapers of Dundalk, Drog-
heda, and other towns? We have no reason to think
that the people of Dunlcer go to Mr. Matthews in any
groater numbers than they do in other places demand-
ing that they be served with Irish manufacture. On
the face of it, we should say that his victory proves
that he pushed Irish manufacture on those of his cus
tomers who, in relation to the Irish Industrial Move-
ment, are more or less indifferent, and they, getting
good value for their money, were quite satisfied.

The victory of Mr. Matthews, of Dunleer, a village
with a population of 480, reflects great honour upon
him, and also reflects anything but honour on the big
drapery houses of such towns as Drogheda and Dun-
dalk. ~What were these houses doing that a drapery
store in the village of Dunleer beat them in this com-
petition ?

Whoover was responsible for the establishment of
this, so far unique competition let loose a new force in
the industrial propaganda. We trust that the pro-
gramme of every local industrial exhibition hencefor-
ward will include a similar competition. And competi-
tion might well be offered to others besides drapers—
tobacconists, for instance. Such a competition for tobac-
conists would be welcome just now when so many weak-
spirited “Irish’’ tobacco retailers are allowing their
windows to be dressed out with the showy empty boxes
of the Combine. We hope our readers will take a note
of the tobacco shops that are playing the anti-Irish
game of the Combine. Tobacco retailers in this coun-
try have a legal right to play the anti-Irish game of the
Combine, and Irish retail purchasers of tobacco have
likewise a legal right to prefer to support those shops
that refuse to walk into the Combine’s cheap shop-
dressing web.

The obnoxious International Exhibition project was
formally introduced to the world to the accompaniment
of a wanton police attack on a number of those who
attended a public meeting. Since then the anti-Irish
project has had a chequered career. Once again the
trolly-pole is off the wire, and there is a _crowd gaping
at the distressed international tram. In the course of
a recent circular from the promoters of this anti-Irish
scheme, we read :— Such an exhibition will most cer-
tainly receive the support of the merchants, producers,
manufacturers, and others from all parts of the world
who are anxious to obtain a portion of the trade of Ire-
land.” It now appears that the very buildings in
which this Dumpers’ Friend is to locate itself are to be
erected as a consequence of some furtive and back-stairs
arrangement by an English firm with a “branch’ in
Dublin. This English firm have guaranteed £25,000
so0 as to bring the total amount up to the figure that
reridors the guarantees of other unhappy people legally
binding. This enterprise, if it ever comes off, is sure to
be subjected to a partial and well-deserved boycott,
and we do not anticipate that the unhappy guarantors
will have much change out of the amounts standing
against their credit.

Mr, James Shanks has a financial interest in the
carrying through of this schemo as well as have the firm
of English contractors that have guarantead £25,000.

CTOBER 21, 1905.

Judge Craig is, no doubt, a wise judge. Addressing
th9 Grand Jury at the Monaghan Quarter Sessions, ho
said, referring to a case of stabbing:—“ It was only by
accident in this case—the mercy of God if anyone liked
to say so—that this man’s lifo was not taken, because
his life was in danger for several days, and the doctor
who attended him so certified.”” “When you stab a man
with a kmﬁa,” said his Honour, you don’t know where
you are going to hit him, and an inch or a quarter of an
inch, or even an eighth of an inch, makes all the differ-
eence between killing a man and only hurting him. For-
tunately the man was only badly hurt.”” When a
quarter of an inch or so saves a man from tho gallows,
what term of jail-life should he got? It is difficult to
geob a clear issue in this country, for if the stabber
turned out to be an Orange lamb, that might alter the
ordinary calculations.

Robert Wilson, “a respectable-looking man,”” of Sil-
verstream, near Monaghan, was charged with wounding
one Robert Mayclim on the 13th of July, the day after
the glorious, pious, and immortal “Twelfth.””  The
injured lamb was returning from the Hand and Pen
Orange Lodge on the morning of the 13th, and the man
\Wilson, who ought to have belonged to the Knife and
Dagger Lodge, used filthy language.  There was an
altercation between the two lambs; there was a kick, a
wrangle, and a stab. On the night of the pious 12th,
the two lambs had a dispute outside the Orange hall
The report in the local paper, the People’s Advocate,
says—*‘‘ They had drink taken.”” The injured lamb de-
posed “ When (he) witness got to the house, he was
about to take out a gun to shoot Wilson, but was pre-
vented.”” What a harmless, innocent pair of bleating
lambs! One Jackson, another lamb amongst other
things, deposed that he (Jackson) “ was the worse of
liquor.”” ~ Dr. James Henry, of Monaghan, deposed
that the-lamb’s (Mayclim) life was in danger for a week.
One James Richie-deposed that he was drunk on the
occasion, and did not see the row. Judge Craig, in his
charge to the jury, condemned witnesses for perjuring
themselves. He concluded :—‘ Any man who has lis-
tened to the evidence in this case fairly and candidly,
and who has brought. his intelligence to bear upon it,
does not need to turn round in the box before convicting
this man of the crime.””

Yet the jury, after forty minutes, could not agree.
Judge Craig asked with righteous indignation—“Is it
because you are Orangemen of the county that you
won’t do your duty?’ His Honour further said:—“1I
think it is a discredit to the Orange Lodge, and to the
society generally, to think that because a brother
Orangeman is charged with committing this offence he
is to get off scot free. If you respect yourselves and the
country in which you live, you will go back and find
this man guilty.”” The judge sent back the jury again
and again, but they refused to convict the Orange lamb.
“Tt is a scandal and an outrage,’’ said the judge.

On the following day the Orange lamb, Wilson,
whose victim'’s life was in danger for a week, was put up
again for trial. Five jurors failed to answer their
names, and were fined £1 each. As a result of the
second trial, the jury found the lamb guilty, but
strongly recommended the ruffian to the mercy of the
Court. And then Judge Craig sentenced this man, who
was found guilty of stabbing another in such a manner
that for a week the life of the latter was in danger, to—
what do you think? Five years? Three years? Only
one year? Nothing of the kind.  Judge Craig sen-
tenced this Orange ruffian to one month's imprisonment
with hard labour. Certainly all who have a propensity
for stabbing their neighbours to within a quarter of an
inch or so of their lives ought to migrate to Monaghan
where Judge Craig doles out sentences.

What does brother the Right Hon. the Earl of Erne,
K.P., H.M.L., Grand Master, think of the “ Hand and
Pon’’ Lodge of Orangemen! What do Brother the
Right Rev. Protestant Bishop Stack, a “Grand Chap-
lain,”” Brother the Right Rev. Protestant Bishop of Os-

. sory, another “Grand Chaplain,” Brother the Right

THE LEADER. 131

Rev. Protestant Bishop of Derry, “ Grand Chaplain,’””
and Brother the Right Rev. Protestant Bishop of
Cashel, Dr. O’Hara, a “ Deputy Grand Chaplain’’ of
the infamous Orange Society, think of the “ Hand and
Pen ’’ (knife) branch? By the way, we hear nothing of
the Grand Orange Bazaar and Fete which it was decided
to hold in November of this year.

An Irish commercial traveller writes:—“1In one of
your recent issues you stated that you were anxious to
hear any little tales or doings of the G. S. Railway
and treatment of passengers. When the G. S. Railway
Company started the corridor trains, they put on a
third class dining car, which, as far as I know, was
largely availed of by the passengers, especially those
travelling by an early morning or evening train. After
some time they discontinued this car for reasons best
known to themselves, and third class passengers who
now wish to take meals while travelling are compelled
to travel second class, and pay difference in fare for
the period they occupy the second class compartment.
For instance, leaving Dublin by the 6.40 morning mail,
a train largely availed of by travellers, if you wish to
take breakfast you are compelled to pay at least the
difference between second and third class fare to the
first stop, which, I believe, is Maryboro’.  This
amounts to something like 2s. 6d., which, along with
price charged for breakfast, makes travelling a little
expensive. I think if the G. S. and W. Railway were
anxious to cater for their third class passengers, who
form a fair proportion of the passenger receipts, if they
found the third class dining car did not pay, might, at
least, allow them to use the second class car while tak-
ing meals. They certainly would not lose by doing
this, as many who heretofore took their meals while
travelling refrain from doing so now owing to the ex-
cessive charge.”’

‘We hope that the resolution passed by the Arch-
bishops and Bishops of Ireland last week at Maynooth
exhorting the ““ National ”’ School managers to have
their teachers trained for the teaching of Irish will be
taken to heart not only by the managers concerned, but
by every person having to do with the education of Irish
Catholics.  The authorities of such places as Castle:
knock, Clongowes, and the Christian Schools, The
Monastery, Tipperary, might, with advantage to the
cause of progress, read the resolution of their lordships
each night before retiring to rest for one week, and each
morning, after breakfast, for another week.

The Bishops, in the interests of Irish education, call
for the appointment on the “National’’ Board of a
majority of Commissioners who “will understand the
educational needs of the country, and be in sympathy
with the principles and sentiments of the mass of the
population.’’

However, Dublin Castle will, we suppose, snap its
fingers at this request. If some of the “Idolators’’
already on the Board were any good, they might, at
least, make things lively.  Is there not an opening for
an active, obstructive policy on the Board that would
bring matters to a head? Is there no mute, inglorious
Parnell amongst the Commissioners who might be in-
duced to make himself famous—and useful? There’s
W. R. J. Molloy, for instance. If he kicked up ruc-
tions at the Board, rendered all-night sittings necessary,
and so carried himself that the police had to be called in,
things would be kept lively, anyway. If a guard of
D.M.P. giants had to be told off to protect Tony
Traill’s whiskers from the passionate clutches of the
Lord Chief Baron’s indignant fingers, and if Stanley
Harrington had to be forcibly restrained from blacking
W. J. M. Starkie’s face with the contents of a pot of
Irish-made paint, the Government might make a con-
cession or two with a view to easing the situation.

The stale old calumny that Pierce and Co., of Wex-
ford, employ all, or nearly all, foreigners, has been
startod again. Under date 27th Mr. David Keating,
of Messrs. Pierce and Co., wrote to the correspondent

3 y_vhﬁ called his attention to the circulation of this story,
- and in the course of his letter he said :— Reverting to

your remark re foreigners employed here, we might
state that out of about five-hundred hands employed
here, there are only five people born foreign to the town.
Two are English, one Welsh, or we might say Cosmopo-

. litan, as they have travelled nearly the whole world.

Another hails from Tipperary, and another from Kerry.
Qutside the five there is not a single employee whose
sire has not seen the Bull Ring, Wexford.”

The Irish Ladies Choral Society, under the conductor-
ship of Miss Donegan, made their first public appear-
ance at the Catholic Truth Society’s Reception last
Thursday night, and their performance carned the
hearty praise of Cardinal Logue, who delivered a short
genial speech at the conclusion of the whole proceedings.
It was a great pity, by the way, that the programme
did not include a few songs in Irish. This omission ‘s
hard to account for, as many of the prominent men of
the Catholic Truth Society are thorough-going Irish Tre-
landers. When a great function, such as this was, is
carried out without as much as one song in Irish, it
gives a bad example to lesser functions throughout the
country. Everybody who is anybody is an Irish Ire-
lander now, but that is no reason why, in the matter of
the language, the will should be too frequently taken for
the deed.

The sick Celtic crow will caw wierdly throughout the
nights of Friday and Saturday, 27th and 28th; the
author of “The Well of the Saints ’’ may have to over-
come a temptation to drown himself. = The Keating
Branch will take possession of the Abbey Theatre on
these nights.  On the first night Mr. Hayes’ popular
play, Seagan na Scuab, will be acted. On Saturday,
28th, for the first time, Father Dinneen’s Cpeaveatn
agup Sopca will be staged.  This latter play, con-
sidered by many to be the author’s best so far, deals
with the Famine times in Kerry. It ought to have
been staged long ago, even though the descendants and
friends of Soupers might not spend a happy hour if
they went to see it. Cneavearh agup Jopca is a sin-
cere play, and there are certain classes of people who
are squeamish of sincerity when the light is turned on
their own breed. They should not, however, be allowed
to obstruct the free growth of Irish dramatic art. We
IOIOk forward with great interest to the staging of this
play.

Amongst the “ Gaelic League Echoes” in our con-
temporary, the Waterford Star, we read :—“ We would
like to see every Gaelic Leaguer invest a shilling in the
“ Philogophy of Irish Ireland,” a series of ably-written
and deeply-thought-out articles contributed some years
ago to the New Ireland Review, by Mr. D. P. Moran,
and now issued in book form under the above title.
Those desirous of meeting the ‘ arguments’ and objec-
tions of certain phonographic prigs, and so-called prac-
tical people, should read the articles on ‘The Future
of the Irish Nation,” the ‘ Pale and the Gael,” and the
‘ Gaelic Revival,” a couple of times a week for six
months. Such a course would strengthen their faith in

" themselves and in the movement for the ro-creation of

an Irish Ireland, and enable them, without much ado,
to send certain ‘ critics’ and small-brained individuals
whose intellectual laziness and consequent inability or
unwillingness to learn Irish sots them sneering at or
“ criticising* the Gaelic movement. We shall give a
few extracts from the ‘ Philosophy ’ in this column now
and again as occasion requires and space permits.
Meantime, those who desire bracing reading, and to ac-
quire faith in themselves and a contempt for all forms of
sham and make-believe, should invest a shilling in the

¢ Philosophy,’ which is published by Messrs. Duffy and
Co., Dublin.”” The second edition—newly printed—of
the volume will be ready in a few days, and orders may
be sent to.Messrs. Duffy and Co., Messrs. Gill and Son,
or to this office.

Messrs. Todd, Burns and Co., Ltd., have issued a
catalogue which has a special interest for supporters of
homo industry. It is practically a concise directory of
the Irish-manufactured articles which the firm keeps
in stock or makes to order. The list is a very compre-
hensive one, and purchasers at the various departments
should find it of much assistance in selecting Irish-made
goods.

The annual concerts in connection with St. Mary’s
Blind Asylum, Merrion, will be held on Wednesday and
Thursday, 25th and 26th inst., in the Round Room,
Rotunda. The programme will be rendered as usual by
the inmates of the institution.

The Evening Telegraph, otherwise Pink, winds up a

THE CATHOLIC TRUTH CONFERENCE.

™ HE Catholic. Truth Society is now not only a
firmly established institution, but happily a
growing institution in Ireland. It came to supply a
real need, and was not one of those many comical “long-
felt wants’’ with whose boisterous birth and speedy,
but silent, demise we are rather familiar in this more or
less non-calculating, but very quick-witted, country.
‘We feel painfully conscious as we write that what we
have to say must be said inside about two columns, and
the five exhaustive papers that have been read covered
such a varied field of inquiry from the Application of
Christianity to the lives of the people, on through Tree-
planting, Primary Education, Municipal Libraries,
down to our friend Mr. Bung, that we feel like resting
content with telling our readers to be on the look-out
for the Annual of the Society, which, in due course,
may be had for sixpence, and for which we bespeak a
generous welcome.

The Officials and Committee are to be congratulated
on the great success of this year’s Conference.  The
new feature was the Reception, and the unqualified suc-

an annual function, is significant of the times. After
all, what is a Reception by a Provost of the discredited,
narrow and so-called University of an anti-Irish exotic
soction compared with a reception in the metropolis of
this Catholic country by one of the country’s Arch-
bishops, and at which the Cardinal Primate of All-
Troland and numerous other Prelates are present? In
a normal state of affairs the latter would be to the
former as a great church to a small sect, as a nation to
a foreign plantation within its boundaries. We are
moving that way. The social star of the exotic breed
and their stale.herring University is declining owing to
the revival of Ireland. ILet us henceforth talk of the
social “and intellectual prestige of the Ivish: Catholic
body. The distracted authorities of the Sourfaco usur-

rivulet for  Idolatrous’’ herrings that may sts,n_d thg

THE N llTIONAL LIBRARY OF IRELAND. 
T 
HERE is fortunately one institution in Ireland 
which that heterogeneous • body called " AJl 
creeds and classes '' really accepts and admires-the 
National Library. 
I ca!1 fancy some narrow-headed 
Irish Irelander-there are such tbi1rgs-waxing sar-
castic over the adjective Natioua-1; but despite all 
sarcasm from implacable cranks, the National Library 
is a good thing, and, I think, the nearest thing to a 
great institution we possess, It has no faults of its 
own; its faults are those of the Government's making, 
apcl they all spring from the paltriness of the dole 
allotted for its maintenance, 
All frishmen have a just reason for resenting the nig-
gardliness of the Government towards the Library, but 
in especial Catholics should resent it, 
Catholics have 
had no provis1on ~ade for their higher education by the 
Government, and to them J;he National Library means 
more than to othe_r creeds. It is the place where stu-
dents preparing for the Royal University have to do 
much of their reading, and it is the only first-class 
goneral library freely open to Catholics, or, indeed, for 
that matter, to anybody, 
The consequence is that the 
p!lrsistent denial of a liberal endowment to the Library 
is a direct and serious injustice to all Catholic students, 
and constitutes an aggravation of 
the injustice 
in-
volved in the refusal of a University for Catholics. The 
opposition of ·t4e British elect~r, and abov~ all, of the 
Nonconformist conscience, is the thing always h~ld up

by “sympathetic’ Conservatives as the reason wh
they. cannot setitle thg Univemity Question. But doez
. the British elector object to the inerease of the annual
grant to .t‘he National Library? And upon what
_gl‘ﬂ“ndF—lf ”he does? What obstreperous part of his
conscience or his “principles ” stands in the way ?

'\Vlmt: is holding them back ? 2t dofdo;
Lllrxf':xs hi_tl'dl)r an exaggeration to say that the National
ary 1s llqlalld s real and true University to-day,
f\nd a Um\‘crslty frequented by all, or almost all, that
15 forward and ‘hopeful in Ireland. T think if T were
a§ked \v_l\nt would lbe the surest way to injure the
higher intellectual life of Ireland just now, I should
say at once, “ Close the National Library.” There are
many things, iucluding even institutions of big preten-
sions, which we could manage, at a pinch, to do with-
out, but the National Library is not one of them. On
all grounds, then, the persistent denial of an adequate
emd_ generous grant. to the Library is an injustice and
an Injury ; an injustice to those whose higher education
depeuds so much upon its efficiency, and an injury to
the higher intellectual life of Ircland.
One does not like to dwell too much upon the politi-
cal or polemical side of things, simply because the life
of Treland savours so much of politics; yet to avoid
these things out of an over-done forbearance is poor-
spirited and contemptible enough.  One does not like
to raise political questions over what should be purely
academic, intellectual affairs; yet since the party in
power is largely composed of men who profess sympathy
with the Catholic claims, and do nothing, one must lay
hold of every stick to beat them with. Their doing no-
thing in a matter where they could do everything is a
deadly proof of the insincerity of their professions.
Another point. All members of the party in power do
not, of course, profess sympathy with the Catholic
claims; they fear, they allege, the restrictive influence
of the priests in education, and would not grant a Ca-
tholic University through dread of the “ limitations of
thought,” which it would mean. Very well. Here is
an institution controlled by themselves—the National
Library. They can choose what books they like for it,
or nominate trustees who will see it done; they can
make it as much “in harmony with modern conditions’’
as they like, without let or hindrance from priest or lay
“ obscurantist.”” Yet what do we find? The objectors
to “ obscurantism ’’ and to ‘limitations of thought >’
are not in the least concerned for the adequate equip-
ment of the National Library. They who think that
the priests would “stint the minds’’ of their charges,
are not ashamed to stint the means available for what
is, perhaps, the only really acceptable, and (to the limit
of its capacity) successful, institution set up by Govern-
ment in Ireland. Everything that can be done to pre-
serve that rank monopoly, Trinity College, is done.
That institution has a magnificent Libary, to which
the public have no access; students of the Royal Uni-
versity have to depead on the National Library, which
the objectors to obscurantism, and the “ sympathetic >’
Unionists, alike conspire to keep in a starved, under-
equipped  condition. =~ When the “sympathetic’’
Unionists make a move towards getting a liberal grant
for the National Library, I shall believe in the sincerity
of their professions, and when the objectors to “ob-
scurantism ’’ make a similar move, I shall believe in the
sincerity of their arguments.

To go into any details of the under-equipment of the
Library is not necessary. Often I have wanted books
there which the Library has not got, but should have,
if it is to fulfil its function as a first-class public library.
The staff of the Library—a very courteous and efficient
staffi—is underpaid, and much of the interior work of
the Library remaing in arrear for want of assistance.
All this, and some minor imperfections, could be reme-
died at once, were the Library only allowed an adequate

ant by Parliament. I consider that it is the duty of
all Irish Members of Parliament to move in this ques-
tion as soon as possible; the Government, left to itself,

- will obviously do nothing. If it should be objected
- that there are no funds available, the answer is easy :

THE LEADER. x

close a Queen’s College—Galway, for choice—and apply
its annual allowance in aid of the National Library.
Home Rule or no Home Rule, University or no Uni-
versity, the National Library cannot be done without,
and it should be amply endowed, and made fully effi-
cient with the least possible delay. IMAAL.

- BELFAST AND BIGOTRY,—VY.

“Two hundred years ago the hand of massacre was
nigh,

And far and wide o’cr Erin’s land was heard the mid-
night cry ;

Now Presbyterian Ulster rests in happiness and peace,

While crimes in distant provinces [rom year to year
mcrease.

Oh! Lord, their bondage quickly turn as streams in
South that flow,

For Popery is what it was two hundred years ago.”’

—Preshyterian Bi-Centenary Ballad.

O less a person than Mr. Gladstone had to confess
himself puzzled when he found that the Ulster
Presbyterians, whose grandfathers had been United
Irishmen, were prepared to line the ditches rather
than submit to his proposed Home Rule Parliament.
Writing to the Dublin Protestant Flome Rule Associa-
tion in 1888, he said—‘‘ I saw with great astonishment
that, on the late visit of Lord Iartington to Belfast,
a number of Protestants assembled to applaud every-
thing that their grandfathers condemned, and to con-
demn everything that their grandfathers applauded.
No town was more devoted a century ago to the prin-
ciple of Trish nationality.” This seeming change of
front T have now to try to account for.

I have submitted that the Volunteers, when they
made their famous demands for Free Trade, etc., were
not Irishmen and did not feel nor think as Irishmen.
And T suggested that that was why their resentment

to be practical. It has not the gimcrackery of an
Academy elected for life to recommend it. No forest
of shadowy initials—no humbug. Would that Mr.
Morris and those who support him had pitched. their
scheme in a low and feasible key, had come down from
the clouds and condescended to walk still longer with
us poor plodding, prosaic every-day mortals; would
that they had not been too ethereal to count the costs
of their visionary project; for shadows cost more than
substances. But, alas! the fates ruled otherwise.

I have a sincere regard for Mr. Morris, and
firmly believe that he is acting sincerely in this matter.
I wish we had many more equally earnest and equally
single-minded. But in this matter he has, I fear, been
ill-advised. There arc persons in the Gaelic move-
ment as in other movements who are remarkable for the
number and variety as well as the delusive splendour
of the schemes they hatch—or rather half-hatch.
Their minds are too restless, they are too fond of excite-
ment to pursue the patience-demanding process of in-
cubation long enough. The patient sitting on their
eggs necessary for avial generation is irksome to them.
When the first fever of incubation has abated they
desert the nest, set about building a new one, and lay-
ing new eggs, with all the flutter and excitement of
abortive but irrepressible philoprogenitiveness.

The examination scheme, or rather shadow of a
scheme, brought before the Ard-Fheis, if it had not
this incorporeal academy tacked on to it, would not

and hence would not attract even the attention of the

the advancement of mankind.

Gambling and drink are unfortunately the only
things that nowadays attract young men to so-called
clubs. *These must be counteracted, and how? Well,
has your correspondent tried the effect of installing a
branch of the Gaelic League in his hall, setting apart
a room or two for the teaching of the language, and
also a room for Ivish dancing. If he has not let him
try, and the result will astonish him, for thoso who
frequent these classes will almost of necessity turn to
his reading and billiard rooms. What he must aim
at is first to induce them to come; second, to induce
them to remain.

Again, a gymuasium worked on proper lines has
been tried to advantage. And how on proper lines?
Well, simply that the apparatus is not there to be
used occasionally by the members, but at stated inter-
vals tournaments midst the members should be started,
or a public exhibition given in the local hall. T may
add I have witnessed some excellent displays of this
kind—excellent too for the financial income of the per-
* forming club.

In a town of my acquaintance of less than four
thousand population a choral class was formed and a
very successful one too, composed of mainly the more
grown of the pupils of the local schools and its former
pupils; a thing more easy of accomplishment nowadays
since singing is an important item in our Primary
Schools’ Programme. But in those days the Gaelic
League was not the power it is now, and perhaps, too,
it also might be left the teaching of choral singing.

Exhibitions of Irish singing and dancing could be
given at various times; then ‘live’’ magic lantern
entertainments illustrating history of local ruins and
monuments, leading on, of course, to broad subject of
our country’s history.

Am I exhausted? No! For there remains that
great lever of success— a good debating society. - How
some smile at the idea a ““ good ’’ debating society in
a country town or village. Well, more than one bril-
liant orator has come forth to fame from such a small
beginning.  The main difficulty is to get a proper
chairman who knows how to conduct a debate—the
local solicitor should be an ideal man for such a posi-
tion from his training and everyday work. He should
know how to deal with his men and keep them to
points at issue. A debating society on proper lines has
been productive of the greatest good.

To conclude: One must not forget that amusement
comes first, everything else after; if we can instruct as
well as amuse, very good, but remember “too far east
is west.”’ Fercus Foam.

FATHER GAYNOR ON IRISH MUSIC.

T has come to pass that people are beginning now to
I wrathfully cry out when the question of Irish
music is broached—a plague on both your houses!
-When attention was first directed towards making a
study of the art of the traditional singer, prejudice
shouted—What, the illiterate peasant !”’ Now the tables
are turned, the cry being instead—‘‘ Not half-bad, but:
not the true old strain.”” Yet anyone who knows how
matters stand recognises that the controversy is only as
yet in its initial stages; anyone who understands has
above all no patience with those who speak as if the
question could be settled off-hand. It cannot; and
though I am prepared to admit that musical matters
in the Gaelic League have, owing to the quarrelling,
come to a stand-still, I comfort myself with thinking
that I, at any rate, have rarely enjoyed it all. Discus-

sions at all times, at summer feiseanna, in the intervals
of concerts, on country ditches, in trams and trains,
oxperiments on fiddles, theorisings on the pipes—these
things come pleasantly back—one grows warm with the

hare recollection of them. ~ Whether the controversy
has led many minds back to first principles in the matter
of singing and music generally may be doubted by some
—a harking back that was absolutely necessary—and
the view that the controversy will all flicker out pre-
sently, that things will go on in a few years time just
ag they did before Dr. Henebry published his pamphlet
may be held by others. But one things is certain, that
it is no wse in saying, as many are beginning to say—
“ Let them settle it between them, but teach us music.””
One who says that simply does ngt know what the fuss
is about. The beginning of musical culture is the fixing
of the (or a) scale in the would-be musician’s mind.
Dr. Henebry says— Fix my scale, thisis the Irish
scale! Father Gaynor says— There is no such scale
to fix!””  There is the central and highest crux. But
if we take Father Gaynor’s views in order, as he deve-
loped them before the Gaclic League of Cork in a recent
lecture, it may conduce to clarity.

The lecturer dealt in opening with recent editions of
Ivish music. He took up O’Neill’s Music of Ireland,
and criticised the first section of it—well, mercilessly 1s
the word. He showed (by singing) that the airs given
down in the book as Irish music were not Irish music at
all.  He sang from O’Neill’s books such well-known
airs as “The Lass of Richmond Hill,”” “Bay of Bis-
cay,”” “Johnny Comes Marching Home Again,”’ etec.
Sometimes these airs were tampered with, but were
always recognisable. Then the book contained some of
Balfe’s, Lover’s, and the well-known ‘‘ Kathleen Ma-
vourneen ’’ of Crouch. Why these airs in a collection
of Irish music? The lecturer then exhibited some
examples of tampering with Irish airs.  That haunt-
ingly beautiful air, ‘O, Breathe Not His Name!”’—an
air that breaks from silence like a few night winds tra-
velling together under starry skies, an earnest whisper-
ing company hurrying into silence again—well, that air
was expanded, embellished! Other airs were padded
with bits of Coon songs, and sometimes touched with
memories of brass-band arrangements. Another com-
plaint against the book was that it contained versions
of some airs not comparable with the received versions
—why perpetuate these inferior editions?  Father
Gaynor’s opinion is that this book must be “ killed.””

Now, Dr. Henebry stood sponsor for this book. He
boomed it. His name is among the list of contributors.
Can Dr. Henebry be trusted then as a guide in matters
relating to Irish music? Culture in music, the power
of discriminating between the dross and the gold, would
have made the book of some value. This musicianship
was wanting.

Father Gaynor took up Dr. Henebry’s own pamphlet.
In his opinion Dr. Henebry does not rightly understand
what a mode in music means, a lack of which knowledge
prevents him from correctly scribing Irish airs. Father
Gaynor would desiderate in an editor of Irish music a
thorough knowledge of plain chant, that is, of modal
music proper, since many of the Irish airs, and some of

- the most characteristic of them, are written in modes

not now in use, and often not studied by modern musi-
cians. According to the lecturer, Dr. Henebry is not
able to fix the mode of a tune; he hence lacks the neces-
sary knowledge for editing Irish music, neither is he
capablo of knowing when Trish music is properly edited.
His own pamphlet, his standing sponsor to O’Neill’s
collection, make him out as ong unfit for the task he has

ertaken. Such is Father Gaynor’s reasoning.

~ Against Dr. Henebry’s assertion that Irish music
has a.‘sca,le all its own, Father Gaynor places his own

- experience. He has written down a large amount of
music directly from the traditional singers; he has
found that thoy always sing “true,” making allow-
ance that is for their quavering voices. A thing he
doubts is whether those who have discovered the Irish
scale are able to take down the airs from the peasants
._pl‘operly, whether they are able to ascribe the tuno to
its proper scale—an wmportant thing, for the taking
down of a tune in a wrong scale would necessitate the
presence of, sometimes, numerous chromatics which,
with other Ivish musicians, he believes to be foreign to
the spirit of our music. Ho took as an example
“ Seaghan Ua Duibhir,” as printed in Clairseach na
nGaedheal. This tune is copied from an older collec-
tion in which chromatics are rightly found, the tune
being put in the wrong scale. As printed in Clainseach
these chromatic notes, essential to the melodies, are
omitted ; the tune is consequently not the tune “Sea-
ghan Ua Duibhir *’ at all.

Father Gaynor’s view of the harmonising of Irish
melodies, briefly, is that such melodies as are not modal
or, more strictly, the modern major or minor modes
can be harmonised according to the ordinary harmonic
laws, but that the “ modal ’’ melodies so called cannot
be so treated. How should these be harmonised? The
harmonist must go back to Palestrina and his school.
‘These harmonised modally: from their works it would
be possible to establish laws that would fit our present
case. Personally it was a relief to me to have this view
of the matter. Those who have been saying that har-
monised Irish melodies are  barbarous,”” and that kind
of thing, remind me of a certain Sourface who wrote
that it was better that the Irish language should die in
its purity than that it should be written ungrammati-
cally. Father Gaynor, however, belicves that there
are very few harmonised versions of Irish airs on the
market worth considering. Brass-band arrangements of
TIrish airs are madness.

Smaller books of Irish music were then examined.
In a less degree they contained a similar class of fault
as O’Neill’s big book. Finally, the lecturer spoke on
the faults of the traditional singer—the undue prolon-
gation of notes, the mistaking of shouting for singing,
the false ornamenting of airs with twists and curls—
imitatory of skilful piping. But the lecturer also
pointed out the virtue of traditional singing—the great
sincerity of it all.

In beginning this rough-and-ready account of a most
interesting lecture, I intended to give my own views of
the great question at issue—the question of an Irish
scale.  Perhaps next week will answer. My views
would be those of a musical layman, if that term con-
veys the same meaning to others as it does me. Finish-
ing, I have to acknowledge a little personal debt to
Father Gaynor. I have to say that he taught me by
means of his Palestrina choir what voice-harmony
meant, a thing the opera singers could not teach me.
All Cork could have learned the lesson, too, but, Cork’s
way, it didn’t! Lzk.

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT—X.

The two statements of fact, and the statement of
“law, here made by Mr. Justice O’Brien are incontro-
vertible, and have most important hearings on the
golution of this question. The trust has been most
clearly and absolutely declared by the deed. The pro-
perty passed by the deed. That the property passed by
the deed is proved (1) by the words of the deed trans-
ferring the property to the trustees; (2) by Erasmus

 Smith’s lease of the property from the trustees; (3) by

the provision that if a license or an Act of Parliament
for incorporating themselves were not obtained within
the space of seven years, it should be lawful for Eras-
‘mus Smith, his heirs, etc., “the premises to re-enter
d the game to have again and repossess as in his
er right.’”” A person cannot re-enter and repossess
roperty which he has not given up to another.
‘Mr. Justice O’Brien’s statement of law is equally in-

controvertible. After the property had passed and the
trusts had been declared, neither had Erasmus Smith,
nor his trustees, nor the Court of Chancery any power
to change them. The Endowment has been a perfect
charitable trust, for in addition to all the essential qua-
lities of a charitable trust enumerated above, it is per-
petual.  When, therefore, the property had passed,
and the trust had been declared, the property had
passed out of the hands of Erasmus Smith beyond the
power of recall, except on a certain contingency, and
the trusts had passed out of his hands beyond the power
of change. It is true that there is a provision in the
Deed that if the parties to it did not procure a license,
or an Act of Parliament, for incorporating themselves
to be a Corporation within the space of seven years, it
should be lawful for Erasmus Smith to repossess the
premises. But the Deed makes special provision, that
the Trustees were to obtain the license or Act of Parlia-
ment only #n case Erasmus Smith had not procured
either. And we know from the 15th Section of the
Act of Settlement, passed in 1662, and from the Bill
sent into England by Erasmus Smith, dated the 13th of
May, 1664, that Erasmus Smith had applied to the
King for this license before the seven years had ex-
pired. His application, then, ought to have barred his
right of resumption. . Moreover, in his Bill of the 13th
of May, 1664, he reiterates his intention of continuing
the lands given for the use of the schools. He, in point
of fact, did not take up the lands. To take them up,
even if he had power to do so, would not suit the game
he was playing. When the property passed and the
trust was declared, the land and trust passed out of the
hands of Erasmus Smith, and came under the caption
of the Court of Chancery.

Tudor, p. 89, says:—“ The Court of Chancery had
in every case jurisdiction by the construction of the
instrument of foundation, whether such instrument were
an Act of Parliament, or a charter, or a deed, or will,
with, or without the assistance of evidence of usage and

other officers, and to regulate by means of schemes the

Again, p. 93, Tudor says:— The Court of Chancery
always had ample jurisdiction to enforco the execution
of charitable trusts. If the charity were established by
‘Ghm:u?l: or Act of Parliament, it would see that the

observed. If it wero founded by a privato individual,
or individuals, it would take care that the trusts de-

Court was not interfered with by the fact that the

principles are illustrated by cases which I nced not

And the property which passed to the 18 Trustees by
the Deed of 1657 was confirmed to them by the King’s
Declaration, dated November, 1668, and the Act of
Settlement 1662. Section 6 of the declaration, and
Section 2 of the Act of Settlement declare that all ad-
venturers were to retain possession of the forfeited lan
of which they were possessed on the 7th of May, 163
And this enactment gave title not only to the adven-
turers who were in actual possession of forfeited lands
on that day, but also to others, such as the Erasmus
Smith Trustees, who had received lands from adven-
turers, and were in possession of them on the 7th
of May, 1659. For in the three certificates of the
Court of Claims, and in the Letters Patent of 1657, it
is quoted as proof of title to forfeited lands, that they
were “in the possession of Erasmus Smith, his Trustees,
or those under whom he or they clayme,’”” on the 7th
of May, 1659. The Trustees, therefore, as Trustees
under the Deed, were the legal owners of the lands
given in Galway, in Roscommon, and in the Isles of
Arran. And Erasmus Smith acted wltra vires in selling
the endowment lands in the Isles of Arran to the Earl
of Arran, even though the 1826 acres in the County of
Limerick, were given by him instead of them. And the
Trustees acted w/tra wires in allowing him to sell those
lands.

The 403 acres given in Roscommon by the Indenture
or Deed have been lost to the Schools, but 253 acres
were given in Sligo by the first Certificate of the Court
of Claims, and by the Letters Patent.

As I have already said, once the trust had been de-
clared by the Deed, Erasmus Smith had no authority
to alter it. This raises and decides an all-important
point on the question of his alleged religious intention.
Either the appointment of the Westminster Catechism
to the schools was one of the trusts appointed by Eras-
mus Smith, or it was.not. If that appointment was
not one of the trusts instituted by Erasmus Smith, it
was no evidence of his religious intention, and the Deed
gives us no evidence of his religious intention. If the
appointment of the Westminster Catechism was ono of
the trusts instituted by Erasmus Smith, he could not
afterwards have changed it and appointed an Episco-

If the religious character impressed on the Erasmus
Smith Endowment by any of the instruments of foun-
dation could make that Endowment, the endowment of
any one religious denomination, the Puritan, anti-
Episcopalian  character impressed on it by the Deed.
would make it the endowment of the Puritans or Pres-

impressed on it by the Deed by which the property
passed out of the hands of Erasmus Smith, and by
which the trusts imposed on that property were de-
clared. And the penal laws which altered that charac-

But neither the religious character impressed on the
Erasmus Smith Endowment by the instruments of
foundation could make that endowment the endowment
of any one religious denomination. For both those
characters were impressed on the endowment, not by
Erasmus Smith, but by the penal laws of his day.
When, therefore, the penal laws were all repealed, the
Endowment remained for the benefit of those to whom
Erasmus Smith had given it. Lord Justice Fitzgibbon
has laid down this principle in his judgment on the
Incorporated Society (Rep. 1885-6, p. 12).

He says:— Funds contributed by the State—that
is, by the whole community—at a time when one de-
nomination of that community was under legal dis-
ability, should become open to all members of that com-
muniby as soon as the legal disability was removed,  ces-
sante ratione cessat lez.””’ This principle has been acted
upon by Lord Justice Fitzgibbon and the other mem-
bers of his Commission in regard to the Royal Schools
Endowment. Erasmus Smith has given his Endow-
ment for the free education of his tenants’ children and
some other puor children specified by him. The State,
and not he, imposed a legal disability on Catholics,
which excluded them from his Endowment. When the
State nas removed that disability, the Endowment
should be open to all including Catholics, ¢ cessante
ratione cessat lex.” The distinction which the Lord Jus-
tice has drawn between endowments of public, and en-
dowments of private origin, does not destroy or lessen
the force of this argument. That distinction would do so
if the disability had been imposed by Erasmus Smith.
But, as I have shown, and will more fully show, the
disability was imposed by the State, and not by Eras-
mus Smith.  Erasmus Smith gave the lands for the
cducation of his tenants, and of some others; the State
gave the penal laws to exclude the Catholics.
